title: The Trump team’s puzzling ‘all is well’ message on the economy
summary:
It was a puzzling and dicey political message before the war; it’s even more puzzling and dicey now.
Today, the message is more: Despite what voters are hearing (or seeing at any local gas station), things are actually quite good.
“We had the best economy in the history of our country my first term,” Trump added.
And perhaps more to the point, Americans said 65%-25% that Trump had worsened economic conditions rather than made them better.
The war has clearly made the economy more of a liability for Trump.

title: One of the most stressful jobs in finance right now: private credit sales
summary:
The private credit boom created a lucrative job on Wall Street: selling the asset class to wealthy individuals hungry for returns.
Anxious clientsInvestors have so many questions that they're now reaching out to their old salespeople for advice, according to one former wholesaler at a major private credit firm who now works in private wealth.
This comes after a sustained hiring boom at the intersection of private wealth and credit.
The ex-wholesaler said he has had former colleagues leave private credit due to redemption limits.
"I have heard this from a number of people: I want old souls managing my private credit," Serio said.

title: Gas prices are sky-high. Here are 8 ways to save at the pump.
summary:
Right now, he thinks he's at a break-even point; within five or so years, should prices remain elevated, he thinks he'll save thousands annually.
Changing gas prices are reshaping American drivers' everyday lives, whether through cutting back on trips or shaking up their budgets.
Consider what grade of gas your vehicle actually needsBennett suggested considering the recommended versus the actual required octane rating.
Check on your vehicleDrivers saving money on gas "really comes down to keeping their vehicle fuel efficient and running properly," Bennett said.
"If gas prices remain this high or continue to surge, then I will continue to avoid driving whenever possible," he said.

title: How Matt Fitzpatrick's PGA Tour win turned up volume in week where money talked
summary:
At least the climax to the PGA Tour's RBC Heritage provided a gripping finale to follow the previous week's thrilling Masters.
"I'm paid so much money to be out there in front of those crowds," Fitzpatrick said.
And after the brilliant shootout birdie that beat Scheffler for his most recent win, Fitzpatrick borrowed an analogy from his other sporting passion, football.
PGA Tour purses would not be so grand had Saudi not pumped so much cash into the rival LIV tour.
Oil money has driven rampant inflation that has made millions for so many golfers, especially in the men's game.
